caveats: a: The BLS staffing pattern pools several unrelated nondurable wholesale industries.
a: O*NET task importance is not task time, wage weight, current automation, or end-to-end substitutability.
a: The frozen labor ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is harmonized separately to the IPS basis.
rho: No direct study measures AI implementation in independent 424930 firms.
rho: Weather shocks, biological variation, and holiday peaks can reduce model reliability exactly when operational stakes are highest.
e: SAF's operation count is an industry estimate with a different, unstated size scope.
e: The frozen 467-firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed list of qualifying firms.
s5: The transaction source covers all nondurable wholesaling and reports only deals known to its marketplace.
s5: There is no exact eligible-firm denominator or observed owner-age distribution.
q: No public study measures pass-through of productivity benefits for floral wholesalers.
q: Capture varies sharply between spot cut-flower sales, recurring florist accounts, nursery programs, hardgoods, and large retail contracts.
d5: Consumer spending is nominal and includes retail channels and seeds outside the exact wholesale construct.
d5: USDA floriculture sales include both retail and wholesale producer sales and omit some nursery and supply categories.
d5: Tariffs, exchange rates, plant disease, and weather could materially alter real volume within five years.
o: The operator-required share differs between fresh cut flowers, nursery stock, potted plants, and nonperishable florist supplies.
o: Consolidation into national importers or retailer distribution centers still preserves an operator but can eliminate the independent local wholesaler.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales, generally earning a wholesale spread and sometimes delivery or service fees; fresh-product value also depends on availability, grading, freshness, conditioning, cold-chain execution, credit, and rapid exception resolution.
lens.excluded_activities: Grower-owned captive sales arms, vertically integrated retailer distribution centers, pure growers, pure retailers, non-inventory brokers, shells, and firms outside the approximately $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent merchant wholesalers repeatedly sourcing, receiving, conditioning, merchandising, holding, selling, and delivering cut flowers, nursery stock, plants, and florist supplies to external florists, garden centers, landscapers, event professionals, institutions, and retailers.

a.bridge: BLS provides employment for the much broader 4241/4247/4249 nondurable-wholesaler group, while O*NET describes generic wholesale-sales tasks. I map sales support, product and availability questions, quoting, contracts, ordering, market monitoring, reports, and replenishment to AI-assistable tasks, then discount heavily for receiving, inspection, conditioning, stocking, cold-chain movement, delivery, and relationship selling. Inclusion of farm-supply and other physical wholesalers may approximate the physical mix, but the absence of 424930 wage weights can bias the estimate either way.
caveats: The BLS staffing pattern pools several unrelated nondurable wholesale industries.
O*NET task importance is not task time, wage weight, current automation, or end-to-end substitutability.
The frozen labor ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is harmonized separately to the IPS basis.
a.change_evidence: A 424930-specific SOC-by-loaded-wage payroll extract and task-time studies from at least eight floral and nursery wholesalers, including current software automation, manual touches per order, shrink handling, receiving inspection, and seasonal labor.
a.rationale: AI can help normalize supplier lists, match substitutions, draft quotes and order responses, forecast event and holiday demand, prioritize aging inventory, reconcile invoices, and triage customer exceptions. The broader BLS occupation data nevertheless show that driving, material moving, and stocking are among the largest jobs, and floral wholesalers require direct quality judgment on perishable, variable product.

d5.bridge: USDA floriculture producer sales and BEA-derived consumer spending measure upstream production and downstream nominal expenditure, not constant-price wholesaler service quantity; neither fully covers nursery stock and florist hardgoods. I infer broadly stable to modestly growing physical demand after removing price effects, with a wide range for housing, events, tariffs, weather, and channel mix. Nominal spending growth likely overstates real quantity, while production value excludes imported product handled by U.S. wholesalers, so the two errors run in opposite directions.
caveats: Consumer spending is nominal and includes retail channels and seeds outside the exact wholesale construct.
USDA floriculture sales include both retail and wholesale producer sales and omit some nursery and supply categories.
Tariffs, exchange rates, plant disease, and weather could materially alter real volume within five years.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of inflation-adjusted 424930 sales, shipment units, and gross margins split by cut flowers, potted plants, nursery stock, and hardgoods, paired with wholesale price indices, import volumes, housing starts, landscaping demand, and event volumes.
d5.rationale: USDA reported 2024 floriculture sales of $6.71 billion, less than 1% above 2023, while SAF's BEA series shows nominal consumer spending on flowers, seeds, and potted plants rising from $68.9 billion in 2023 to $71.0 billion in 2024. The evidence supports durable consumer and commercial use but little basis for strong real growth. Nursery, landscaping, weddings, funerals, holidays, and institutional demand diversify the basket, while housing cycles and discretionary spending add volatility.

caveats: SAF's operation count is an industry estimate with a different, unstated size scope.
The frozen 467-firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed list of qualifying firms.
e.change_evidence: A verified roster of the estimated band firms coded for independent ownership, external-customer share, repeat revenue, normalized EBITDA, cold-chain and delivery capabilities, seasonality, customer concentration, and owner dependence.
e.rationale: Independent wholesalers usually provide repeat external sourcing, availability, credit, conditioning, and delivery, closely matching the recurring-service lens. Exclusions arise from captive grower or retailer operations, commodity brokers with no accountable service layer, seasonal businesses without transferable year-round systems, and owner-dependent firms. The Society of American Florists' estimate of 400 wholesale operations versus the frozen 467 band estimate also warns that classification and size-band boundaries are imprecise.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 424930 ($2,505,705,427) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 424930 ($20,552,620,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1137 (margins.json[42]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Retail (Distributors) EBITDA/Sales 11.37%); revenue/employee $395,334 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 424930); class <5: 1,679 firms, avg revenue/firm $934,328 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $106,233 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 501 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,634,994 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $299,599 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 377 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,700,164 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $534,409 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 379 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,031,478 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,367,979 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $56,393,886 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,411,985 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $289,509,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,917,173 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 467

caveats: The operator-required share differs between fresh cut flowers, nursery stock, potted plants, and nonperishable florist supplies.
Consolidation into national importers or retailer distribution centers still preserves an operator but can eliminate the independent local wholesaler.
o.change_evidence: Channel-flow data quantifying grower-direct, importer-direct, wholesaler-mediated, marketplace, and retailer-integrated volume by product, plus customer interviews testing which procurement, quality, credit, and logistics functions could actually be self-served.
o.rationale: Perishable and biologically variable products continue to require accountable sourcing, inspection, conditioning, inventory allocation, cold-chain handling, delivery, credit, and exception resolution. USDA Market News itself organizes current price, volume, quality, and condition information using contacts across buyers and sellers, illustrating the operational variables involved. Direct grower-to-retailer programs and digital ordering can bypass some wholesalers, but software alone cannot receive, preserve, grade, and deliver the physical product.

caveats: No public study measures pass-through of productivity benefits for floral wholesalers.
Capture varies sharply between spot cut-flower sales, recurring florist accounts, nursery programs, hardgoods, and large retail contracts.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice analysis by customer segment showing renewal or bid cadence, markup and rebate structures, freight and shrink allocation, price-match behavior, customer churn, and gross-margin retention after measured operational improvements.
q.rationale: Wholesale spreads and service-intensive local relationships allow part of internally generated productivity, lower shrink, and improved purchasing to remain with the operator. Price transparency, rapid competitor substitution, powerful event and retail buyers, and frequent repricing transmit some gains. Improvements in freshness, fill rate, working capital, and avoided spoilage should be more retainable than visible reductions in sales-support cost.

caveats: No direct study measures AI implementation in independent 424930 firms.
Weather shocks, biological variation, and holiday peaks can reduce model reliability exactly when operational stakes are highest.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month pre/post records from wholesaler ERP, e-commerce, warehouse, route, and customer-service systems showing user adoption, straight-through order rates, override rates, shrink, fill rate, labor hours, errors, and avoided hires.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible for quoting, order capture, substitution suggestions, purchasing support, route and inventory prioritization, collections, and customer-service drafting. Realization is limited by fragmented supplier/customer systems, informal communication, volatile availability, seasonality, imperfect item identifiers, perishability, and the requirement that experienced people approve quality, substitutions, allocations, and credit decisions.

caveats: The transaction source covers all nondurable wholesaling and reports only deals known to its marketplace.
There is no exact eligible-firm denominator or observed owner-age distribution.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of 424930-matched closed deals from BizBuySell, Axial, GF Data, broker CRM systems, SBA 7(a) records, and owner interviews covering age, succession plans, lease and facility transferability, and supplier/customer consent requirements.
s5.rationale: A roughly one-in-five five-year transfer probability reflects a fragmented, relationship-based population with plausible succession-driven supply but meaningful transfer friction from perishability, working capital, facilities, owner relationships, and supplier credit. BizBuySell's 16 reported nondurable-wholesaler transactions in 2025 demonstrate some liquidity but do not establish a 424930 transfer rate.

principal_driver: Repeat ordering plus costly perishability creates a strong case for AI-assisted forecasting, substitution, and exception management around an operator that remains physically necessary.
principal_weakness: The low labor ratio and irreducible handling, delivery, and quality-control work limit how much AI exposure can become removable labor.

run_meta.title: Flower, Nursery Stock, and Florists' Supplies Merchant Wholesalers

sources.supports: For the broader 4241/4247/4249 group, identifies the largest occupations including 66,350 nontechnical sales representatives, 56,820 heavy-truck drivers, 45,270 material movers, 25,980 stockers/order fillers, 15,980 office clerks, and 15,630 customer-service representatives.
sources.title: BLS Data Tables for OEWS Industry Charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Lists wholesale-sales tasks including answering product and availability questions, recommending products, quoting terms and delivery dates, preparing contracts and orders, monitoring markets, reporting, checking stock, and forwarding orders.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Sales Representatives, Wholesale and Manufacturing, Except Technical and Scientific Products

sources.supports: Explains that reporters gather and publish current wholesale-market information on price, volume, quality, and condition using direct contacts with sales personnel, suppliers, brokers, and buyers.
sources.title: USDA Agricultural Marketing Service: Specialty Crops Market News

sources.supports: Reports BEA-derived nominal spending on flowers, seeds, and potted plants of $71.0 billion in 2024 versus $68.9 billion in 2023, estimates 400 floral wholesale operations, and reports 11,262 domestic producers and $6.71 billion of floriculture sales in 2024.
sources.title: Society of American Florists: Floral Industry Facts

sources.supports: Reports 16 nondurable-goods wholesaler/distributor transactions for 2025, with median sale price $525,000, median revenue $1,301,710, and median cash flow $225,000.
sources.title: BizBuySell 2026 First-Quarter Insight Report: Full-Year 2025 Data Tables

sources.supports: Reports 11,262 producers and $6.71 billion of floriculture sales in 2024, less than 1% above 2023; defines included fresh cut flowers, potted plants, bedding and garden plants, perennials, greens, and propagative material and states that sales include retail and wholesale.
sources.title: USDA NASS 2024 Floriculture Crops Highlights

sources.supports: Reports $18.3 billion in 2024 floriculture, nursery, and specialty-crop sales across 23,060 U.S. horticulture operations.
sources.title: USDA NASS: U.S. Horticulture Operations Report $18.3 Billion in Sales

title: Flower, Nursery Stock, and Florists' Supplies Merchant Wholesalers
